query: The work referenced in footnote 397 of Federico Lauria's 2014 dissertation is also the source for the titles of two paintings in the Smithsonian American Art Museum's collection, as of August 2023. What is the absolute difference between the chapter numbers of the chapters that the titles of these two paintings quote?

The query asks for the **absolute difference between the chapter numbers** of two specific chapters in Federico Lauria's 2014 dissertation. These chapters are identified by the titles of two paintings held in the Smithsonian American Art Museum's collection (as of August 2023). The solution requires locating the paintings, identifying their titles, finding the corresponding chapters in the dissertation, and calculating the numerical difference.

| Skill / Tool | Explanation of Utility |

| **Generalist_Solution_Generator_Tool** | **Primary Strategy:** This tool is best suited for the initial breakdown of the complex query. It allows for precise retrieval of the specific text needed to calculate the difference. |
| **Wikipedia_RAG_Search_Tool** | **Verification:** While the query relies on specific academic data, this tool can be used to cross-reference the titles of the paintings or the specific chapters if the RAG tool returns ambiguous results. Federico Lauria's 2014 dissertation is titled "The Logic of the Liver. A Deontic View of the Intentionality of Desire"[1](https://philpeople.org/profiles/federico-lauria), [2](https://www.academia.edu/33030870/The_Logic_of_the_Liver_A_Deontic_View_of_the_Intentionality_of_Desire), [3](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Federico-Lauria). He completed his Ph.D. at the University of Geneva in 2014[1](https://philpeople.org/profiles/federico-lauria), [4](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/369033002_Desiderative_Truth_Caprice_and_the_Flaws_of_Desire).

The dissertation examines the intentionality of desire, proposing a "deontic alternative" inspired by Meinong, in contrast to the two classical views that understand desire as either a positive evaluation or a disposition to act[1](https://philpeople.org/profiles/federico-lauria), [4](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/369033002_Desiderative_Truth_Caprice_and_the_Flaws_of_Desire), [3](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Federico-Lauria). Lauria's deontic view suggests that desiring involves representing a state of affairs as "what ought to be" or "what should be"[1](https://philpeople.org/profiles/federico-lauria), [2](https://www.academia.edu/33030870/The_Logic_of_the_Liver_A_Deontic_View_of_the_Intentionality_of_Desire), [5](https://sites.cisa-unige.ch/ronald-de-sousa/assets/pdf/Lauria_Paper.pdf). This perspective argues that a norm of the ought-to-be type is central to the intentional mode of desire[1](https://philpeople.org/profiles/federico-lauria).

The work is structured in three parts and aims to satisfy key desiderata for a theory of desire's intentionality, including the "world-to-mind direction of fit," the grounding of norms on values, and the "death of desire principle" (the idea that one cannot desire what one perceives as actual)[1](https://philpeople.org/profiles/federico-lauria), [2](https://www.academia.edu/33030870/The_Logic_of_the_Liver_A_Deontic_View_of_the_Intentionality_of_Desire). It also explores different types of desires and addresses the correctness or appropriateness of desires, arguing for "deontic truth" as the standard for desiderative truth[1](https://philpeople.org/profiles/federico-lauria), [2](https://www.academia.edu/33030870/The_Logic_of_the_Liver_A_Deontic_View_of_the_Intentionality_of_Desire), [5](https://sites.cisa-unige.ch/ronald-de-sousa/assets/pdf/Lauria_Paper.pdf).
